meaning_kannada: Active ಮತ್ತು Passive Voice ಮೂಲ ನಿಯಮ - ಕರ್ತೃ ಮತ್ತು ಕರ್ಮ ಪದಗಳ ಬದಲಾವಣೆ.
meaning_english:
The subject of the Active Voice becomes the object of the Passive Voice, and the object of the Active Voice becomes the subject of the Passive Voice.

• Structure: Subject + Verb + Object (Active) ➔ Object + Helping Verb + V3 + by + Subject (Passive).
• Example: 'He writes a letter' (AV) ➔ 'A letter is written by him' (PV).
• Tip: Only Transitive verbs (verbs with an object) can be changed into passive voice.

When the subject moves to the object position in Passive Voice, the pronoun form changes.

• I ➔ me
• We ➔ us
• You ➔ you
• He ➔ him
• She ➔ her
• It ➔ it
• They ➔ them
• Tip: If the subject is 'Someone', 'People', or 'Police', 'by + subject' is often omitted in Passive Voice.

In Simple Present Tense, use helping verbs 'is', 'am', or 'are' followed by the past participle (V3).

• Pattern: Object + is/am/are + V3 + by + Subject.
• Active: She sings a song.
• Passive: A song is sung by her.
• Exam Tip: Check for the V3 form of the verb immediately after the helping verb.

In Simple Past Tense, use 'was' or 'were' according to the number of the new subject (Object).

• Pattern: Object + was/were + V3 + by + Subject.
• Active: They built a house.
• Passive: A house was built by them.
• Example: He invited me ➔ I was invited by him.

When the sentence is in continuous form (V+ing), use 'being' in the passive form.

• Pattern: Object + is/am/are + being + V3 + by + Subject.
• Active: I am writing a story.
• Passive: A story is being written by me.
• Exam Tip: If you see '-ing' in Active Voice, look for 'being' in the Passive options.

Use 'was being' or 'were being' based on the singular/plural status of the object.

• Pattern: Object + was/were + being + V3 + by + Subject.
• Active: She was cooking food.
• Passive: Food was being cooked by her.

meaning_kannada: ಪೂರ್ಣ ವರ್ತಮಾನ ಕಾಲದ ನಿಯಮ (has/have + been + V3).
meaning_english:
In perfect tenses, the word 'been' is mandatory between the helping verb and the main verb.

• Pattern: Object + has/have + been + V3 + by + Subject.
• Active: He has finished the work.
• Passive: The work has been finished by him.
• Tip: 'Has' for singular objects, 'Have' for plural objects.

Regardless of singular or plural, use 'had been' in past perfect passive voice.

• Pattern: Object + had + been + V3 + by + Subject.
• Active: They had won the match.
• Passive: The match had been won by them.

Use 'will be' or 'shall be' followed by V3.

• Pattern: Object + will be + V3 + by + Subject.
• Active: She will write an exam.
• Passive: An exam will be written by her.
• Note: Future Continuous and all Perfect Continuous tenses usually do not have a passive form.

meaning_kannada: Modal Verbs (Can, May, Should, Must) ನಿಯಮ.
meaning_english:
For modals, simply add 'be' after the modal verb in the passive form.

• Pattern: Object + Modal + be + V3 + by + Subject.
• Active: You must follow the rules.
• Passive: The rules must be followed by you.
• Example: I can do it ➔ It can be done by me.

Sentences that give an order or command use 'Let' in passive voice.

• Pattern: Let + Object + be + V3.
• Active: Close the door.
• Passive: Let the door be closed.
• Alternative: You are ordered to close the door.

When 'Please' or 'Kindly' is used, replace it with 'You are requested to' in the passive form.

• Active: Please sit down.
• Passive: You are requested to sit down.
• Note: The verb after 'to' remains in V1 form.

Questions starting with 'Who' change to 'By whom' in passive voice.

• Active: Who wrote this letter?
• Passive: By whom was this letter written?
• Tip: Always check if the question mark is maintained in the passive form.

If a sentence has two objects, either can become the subject. Usually, the Indirect Object (person) is preferred.

• Active: He gave me a book.
• Passive 1: I was given a book by him.
• Passive 2: A book was given to me by him.

If a verb is followed by a preposition in the active voice, keep that preposition with the verb in the passive voice too.

• Active: They laughed at the beggar.
• Passive: The beggar was laughed at by them.
• Common Error: Do not remove 'at' even if 'by' follows it.
